title: The Locksmith's Corner
text_zh: 小区门口有一个配钥匙的摊位。摊主是一个五十多岁的男人，头发花白，戴着一副老式眼镜。他的摊位很小，只有一张折叠桌和一台磨钥匙的机器。周围挂满了各种各样的空白钥匙，像一串串小小的风铃。大多数时候他都没有客人，就坐在那里看报纸或者听收音机。偶尔有人来配钥匙，他接过旧钥匙仔细看一眼，不说话，选一把空白的，放进机器里。几分钟以后一把新钥匙就好了。他把两把钥匙一起交给客人，说「试一下」。我搬来这个小区三年了，他一直在那个角落。不管春夏秋冬，不管有没有人来，他就在那里。我有时候想，他守的不是一个摊位，而是一种正在消失的生活节奏——不着急，不竞争，只要有人需要就够了。
text_pinyin: Xiǎoqū ménkǒu yǒu yí gè pèi yàoshi de tānwèi. Tānzhǔ shì yí gè wǔshí duō suì de nánrén, tóufa huābái, dài zhe yí fù lǎoshì yǎnjìng. Tā de tānwèi hěn xiǎo, zhǐ yǒu yì zhāng zhédié zhuō hé yì tái mó yàoshi de jīqì. Zhōuwéi guà mǎn le gè zhǒng gè yàng de kòngbái yàoshi, xiàng yí chuàn chuàn xiǎoxiǎo de fēnglíng. Dà duōshù shíhou tā dōu méiyǒu kèrén, jiù zuò zài nàlǐ kàn bàozhǐ huòzhě tīng shōuyīnjī. Ǒu'ěr yǒu rén lái pèi yàoshi, tā jiē guò jiù yàoshi zǐxì kàn yì yǎn, bù shuōhuà, xuǎn yì bǎ kòngbái de, fàng jìn jīqì lǐ. Jǐ fēnzhōng yǐhòu yì bǎ xīn yàoshi jiù hǎo le. Tā bǎ liǎng bǎ yàoshi yìqǐ jiāo gěi kèrén, shuō「shì yíxià」. Wǒ bān lái zhège xiǎoqū sān nián le, tā yìzhí zài nàge jiǎoluò. Bùguǎn chūn xià qiū dōng, bùguǎn yǒu méiyǒu rén lái, tā jiù zài nàlǐ. Wǒ yǒu shíhou xiǎng, tā shǒu de bú shì yí gè tānwèi, ér shì yì zhǒng zhèngzài xiāoshī de shēnghuó jiézòu — bù zháojí, bù jìngzhēng, zhǐyào yǒu rén xūyào jiù gòu le.
text_en: At the entrance of the residential compound there's a key-cutting stall. The owner is a man in his fifties with graying hair and old-fashioned glasses. His stall is small — just a folding table and a key-grinding machine. Blank keys of all kinds hang around it like strings of tiny wind chimes. Most of the time he has no customers and just sits reading the newspaper or listening to the radio. Occasionally someone comes to get a key copied. He takes the old key, examines it carefully without speaking, selects a blank one, and puts it in the machine. A few minutes later the new key is ready. He hands both keys to the customer and says 'try it.' I've lived in this compound for three years and he's always been in that corner. Rain or shine, customers or not, he's just there. Sometimes I think what he's guarding isn't a stall but a disappearing rhythm of life — unhurried, uncompetitive, enough as long as someone needs him.

questions.q_zh: 摊主大多数时候在做什么？
questions.q_en: What does the stall owner do most of the time?

options.text_en: Reads newspapers or listens to the radio

questions.q_zh: 空白钥匙被比作什么？
questions.q_en: What are the blank keys compared to?

questions.q_zh: 作者认为摊主守护的是什么？
questions.q_en: What does the author think the stall owner is guarding?

options.pinyin: yí fèn gāo shōurù de gōngzuò

options.text: 一种正在消失的生活节奏
options.pinyin: yì zhǒng zhèngzài xiāoshī de shēnghuó jiézòu

title: Morning Tai Chi in the Park
text_zh: 每天早上六点，公园里就开始热闹了，但是一种很安静的热闹。最东边的角落属于太极拳的人。他们大多是退休的老人，穿着宽松的衣服，跟着一个白头发的师傅慢慢地移动。从远处看，他们像是在水底下行走——每一个动作都有阻力，但看起来毫不费力。有一个阿姨总是站在最后面，动作比别人慢半拍，但她脸上的表情最专注。我问过师傅为什么太极拳要那么慢。他说「慢才能感觉到自己的身体，快的话你只是在做动作，慢的话你才是在练功夫」。旁边广场舞的音乐声很大，但太极的角落好像有一个隐形的墙，把噪音挡在外面。那种安静不是没有声音，而是内心的安静。
text_pinyin: Měi tiān zǎoshang liù diǎn, gōngyuán lǐ jiù kāishǐ rènao le, dànshì yì zhǒng hěn ānjìng de rènao. Zuì dōngbiān de jiǎoluò shǔyú tàijíquán de rén. Tāmen dà duō shì tuìxiū de lǎorén, chuān zhe kuānsōng de yīfu, gēn zhe yí gè bái tóufa de shīfu mànmàn de yídòng. Cóng yuǎnchù kàn, tāmen xiàng shì zài shuǐ dǐxia xíngzǒu — měi yí gè dòngzuò dōu yǒu zǔlì, dàn kàn qǐlái háo bú fèilì. Yǒu yí gè āyí zǒng shì zhàn zài zuì hòumiàn, dòngzuò bǐ biérén màn bàn pāi, dàn tā liǎn shàng de biǎoqíng zuì zhuānzhù. Wǒ wèn guò shīfu wèi shénme tàijíquán yào nàme màn. Tā shuō「màn cái néng gǎnjué dào zìjǐ de shēntǐ, kuài de huà nǐ zhǐ shì zài zuò dòngzuò, màn de huà nǐ cái shì zài liàn gōngfu」. Pángbiān guǎngchǎng wǔ de yīnyuè shēng hěn dà, dàn tàijí de jiǎoluò hǎoxiàng yǒu yí gè yǐnxíng de qiáng, bǎ zàoyīn dǎng zài wàimiàn. Nà zhǒng ānjìng bú shì méiyǒu shēngyīn, ér shì nèixīn de ānjìng.
text_en: Every morning at six the park comes alive, but with a very quiet kind of liveliness. The eastern corner belongs to the tai chi people. They're mostly retired elderly, wearing loose clothing, following a white-haired master in slow movements. From a distance they look like they're walking underwater — every movement meets resistance yet looks effortless. One auntie always stands at the very back, half a beat behind everyone else, but with the most focused expression on her face. I once asked the master why tai chi has to be so slow. He said 'only when you're slow can you feel your own body; when you're fast you're just going through motions, when you're slow you're actually practicing kung fu.' The square dance music blares nearby, but the tai chi corner seems to have an invisible wall keeping the noise out. That kind of quiet isn't the absence of sound — it's inner stillness.

questions.q_zh: 公园里的早晨是什么样的热闹？
questions.q_en: What kind of liveliness does the park have in the morning?

options.text_en: A very quiet kind of liveliness

questions.q_zh: 师傅说慢有什么好处？
questions.q_en: What does the master say is the benefit of slowness?

options.text: 慢才能感觉到自己的身体
options.pinyin: màn cái néng gǎnjué dào zìjǐ de shēntǐ
options.text_en: Only when slow can you feel your own body

options.pinyin: lǎoniánrén bù néng dòng tài kuài

questions.q_zh: 作者说太极角落的安静是什么意思？
questions.q_en: What does the author mean by the quiet of the tai chi corner?

title: The Convenience Store at 3 AM
text_zh: 凌晨三点的便利店是一个奇怪的世界。灯光特别亮，和外面的黑夜形成强烈的对比。店员是一个年轻的女孩，看起来不困，在整理货架上的饮料。我买了一杯热咖啡和一个三明治。她扫完码以后说了一句「这么晚还没睡啊」，语气不是关心也不是好奇，只是一种轻轻的确认——我们都是在这个不正常的时间里醒着的人。柜台旁边坐着一个穿制服的快递员，低着头吃盒饭。角落里有一个学生在看书，面前放着两罐空的咖啡。窗外偶尔有一辆出租车经过。这里没有人认识谁，也没有人需要假装什么。凌晨三点的便利店是城市里最诚实的地方——每个人都是因为各自的原因还没有回家，而这盏灯为所有人亮着。
text_pinyin: Língchén sān diǎn de biànlìdiàn shì yí gè qíguài de shìjiè. Dēngguāng tèbié liàng, hé wàimiàn de hēiyè xíngchéng qiángliè de duìbǐ. Diànyuán shì yí gè niánqīng de nǚhái, kàn qǐlái bú kùn, zài zhěnglǐ huòjià shàng de yǐnliào. Wǒ mǎi le yì bēi rè kāfēi hé yí gè sānmíngzhì. Tā sǎo wán mǎ yǐhòu shuō le yí jù「zhème wǎn hái méi shuì a」, yǔqì bú shì guānxīn yě bú shì hàoqí, zhǐ shì yì zhǒng qīngqīng de quèrèn — wǒmen dōu shì zài zhège bú zhèngcháng de shíjiān lǐ xǐng zhe de rén. Guìtái pángbiān zuò zhe yí gè chuān zhìfú de kuàidìyuán, dī zhe tóu chī héfàn. Jiǎoluò lǐ yǒu yí gè xuéshēng zài kàn shū, miànqián fàng zhe liǎng guàn kōng de kāfēi. Chuāng wài ǒu'ěr yǒu yí liàng chūzūchē jīngguò. Zhèlǐ méiyǒu rén rènshi shéi, yě méiyǒu rén xūyào jiǎzhuāng shénme. Língchén sān diǎn de biànlìdiàn shì chéngshì lǐ zuì chéngshí de dìfāng — měi gè rén dōu shì yīnwèi gèzì de yuányīn hái méiyǒu huí jiā, ér zhè zhǎn dēng wèi suǒyǒu rén liàng zhe.
text_en: The convenience store at 3 AM is a strange world. The lights are exceptionally bright, forming a stark contrast with the darkness outside. The clerk is a young woman who doesn't look sleepy, organizing drinks on the shelf. I bought a hot coffee and a sandwich. After scanning the code she said 'still not asleep this late, huh' — not with concern or curiosity, just a light acknowledgment — we're both people awake at this abnormal hour. Beside the counter sits a delivery worker in uniform, head down eating a boxed meal. In the corner a student reads, two empty coffee cans in front of them. Occasionally a taxi passes outside the window. Nobody here knows anyone, and nobody needs to pretend to be anything. The convenience store at 3 AM is the most honest place in the city — everyone is here because of their own reasons for not going home, and this light stays on for all of them.

questions.q_zh: 店员说话的语气是什么样的？
questions.q_en: What is the tone of the clerk's remark?

questions.q_zh: 便利店里有哪些人？
questions.q_en: Who is in the convenience store?

questions.q_zh: 作者为什么说便利店是「最诚实的地方」？
questions.q_en: Why does the author call the store 'the most honest place'?

options.text: 因为没有人需要假装什么
options.pinyin: yīnwèi méiyǒu rén xūyào jiǎzhuāng shénme
options.text_en: Because nobody needs to pretend to be anything

title: The Library's Unwritten Rules
text_zh: 我常去的图书馆有很多没有人写下来的规矩。比如，靠窗的位置永远属于那几个考研的学生，他们早上八点就来了，书包和水杯标记了领地。比如，二楼东边是安静区，就算没有贴牌子，新来的人也会自然地降低音量。再比如，自习室的插座旁边的位置最受欢迎，大家会把充电器插上然后绕很远的线。还有一个有趣的现象：如果一个人去洗手间或者去买水，把书留在座位上就表示「我还会回来」，没有人会坐那个位置。但是如果超过一个小时，旁边的人就会开始犹豫：这个人到底还来不来？这些规矩没有任何人制定，但大家都默默地遵守。我觉得这就是公共空间最好的样子——不需要管理者，只需要默契。
text_pinyin: Wǒ cháng qù de túshūguǎn yǒu hěn duō méiyǒu rén xiě xiàlái de guījǔ. Bǐrú, kào chuāng de wèizhi yǒngyuǎn shǔyú nà jǐ gè kǎoyán de xuéshēng, tāmen zǎoshang bā diǎn jiù lái le, shūbāo hé shuǐbēi biāojì le lǐngdì. Bǐrú, èr lóu dōngbiān shì ānjìng qū, jiùsuàn méiyǒu tiē páizi, xīn lái de rén yě huì zìrán de jiàngdī yīnliàng. Zài bǐrú, zìxíshì de chāzuò pángbiān de wèizhi zuì shòu huānyíng, dàjiā huì bǎ chōngdiànqì chā shàng ránhòu rào hěn yuǎn de xiàn. Hái yǒu yí gè yǒuqù de xiànxiàng: rúguǒ yí gè rén qù xǐshǒujiān huòzhě qù mǎi shuǐ, bǎ shū liú zài zuòwèi shàng jiù biǎoshì「wǒ hái huì huílái」, méiyǒu rén huì zuò nàge wèizhi. Dànshì rúguǒ chāoguò yí gè xiǎoshí, pángbiān de rén jiù huì kāishǐ yóuyù: zhège rén dàodǐ hái lái bu lái? Zhèxiē guījǔ méiyǒu rènhé rén zhìdìng, dàn dàjiā dōu mòmò de zūnshǒu. Wǒ juéde zhè jiù shì gōnggòng kōngjiān zuì hǎo de yàngzi — bù xūyào guǎnlǐzhě, zhǐ xūyào mòqì.
text_en: The library I often go to has many unwritten rules. For example, the window seats always belong to those few grad-school-exam students who arrive by eight in the morning, their backpacks and water bottles marking territory. The east side of the second floor is the quiet zone — even without a sign, newcomers naturally lower their voices. The seats near the outlets in the study room are the most popular; people plug in their chargers and run cables a long way. There's also an interesting phenomenon: if someone goes to the restroom or to buy water and leaves their books on the seat, it means 'I'll be back,' and nobody will take that seat. But if more than an hour passes, the people nearby start to wonder: is this person actually coming back? Nobody established these rules, but everyone silently follows them. I think this is what a public space looks like at its best — no managers needed, just mutual understanding.

questions.q_zh: 靠窗的位置属于谁？
questions.q_en: Who do the window seats belong to?

questions.q_zh: 把书留在座位上表示什么？
questions.q_en: What does leaving books on a seat mean?

questions.q_zh: 作者觉得公共空间最好的样子是什么？
questions.q_en: What does the author think a public space looks like at its best?

options.pinyin: yǒu hěn duō guīdìng hé biāoyǔ

options.text: 不需要管理者，只需要默契
options.pinyin: bù xūyào guǎnlǐzhě, zhǐ xūyào mòqì
options.text_en: No managers needed, just mutual understanding

options.pinyin: měi gè rén dōu bù shuōhuà

title: The Hospital Elevator
text_zh: 医院的电梯和其他地方的电梯不一样。首先它特别大，要能装下轮椅和病床。其次按钮的高度比较低，坐在轮椅上的人也能够得到。最不一样的是里面的气氛。在商场的电梯里，人们会看手机、聊天、笑。在医院的电梯里，大多数人沉默。有的人手里拿着检查报告，有的人眼睛红红的，有的人推着轮椅上的家人。每一层楼停下来，进来或出去的人脸上都写着不同的故事。四楼是产科——偶尔有人抱着新生儿笑着出来，那是整个电梯里最温暖的时刻。六楼以上是住院部——按那些楼层的人往往表情比较严肃。我注意到一个细节：在医院的电梯里，人们会主动帮别人按楼层、扶门。也许是因为在这个地方，每个人都更容易理解什么叫脆弱。
text_pinyin: Yīyuàn de diàntī hé qítā dìfāng de diàntī bù yíyàng. Shǒuxiān tā tèbié dà, yào néng zhuāng xià lúnyǐ hé bìngchuáng. Qícì ànniǔ de gāodù bǐjiào dī, zuò zài lúnyǐ shàng de rén yě néng gòu de dào. Zuì bù yíyàng de shì lǐmiàn de qìfēn. Zài shāngchǎng de diàntī lǐ, rénmen huì kàn shǒujī, liáotiān, xiào. Zài yīyuàn de diàntī lǐ, dà duōshù rén chénmò. Yǒu de rén shǒu lǐ ná zhe jiǎnchá bàogào, yǒu de rén yǎnjing hóng hóng de, yǒu de rén tuī zhe lúnyǐ shàng de jiārén. Měi yì céng lóu tíng xiàlái, jìn lái huò chūqù de rén liǎn shàng dōu xiě zhe bùtóng de gùshi. Sì lóu shì chǎnkē — ǒu'ěr yǒu rén bào zhe xīnshēng'ér xiào zhe chūlái, nà shì zhěnggè diàntī lǐ zuì wēnnuǎn de shíkè. Liù lóu yǐshàng shì zhùyuàn bù — àn nàxiē lóucéng de rén wǎngwǎng biǎoqíng bǐjiào yánsù. Wǒ zhùyì dào yí gè xìjié: zài yīyuàn de diàntī lǐ, rénmen huì zhǔdòng bāng biérén àn lóucéng, fú mén. Yěxǔ shì yīnwèi zài zhège dìfāng, měi gè rén dōu gèng róngyì lǐjiě shénme jiào cuìruò.
text_en: Hospital elevators are different from elevators elsewhere. First, they're especially large — they need to fit wheelchairs and hospital beds. Second, the buttons are lower so people in wheelchairs can reach them. What's most different is the atmosphere inside. In a shopping mall elevator, people check phones, chat, laugh. In a hospital elevator, most people are silent. Some hold examination reports, some have red eyes, some push family members in wheelchairs. At each floor, the people entering or exiting carry different stories on their faces. The fourth floor is maternity — occasionally someone comes out smiling with a newborn, and that's the warmest moment in the whole elevator. The sixth floor and above is the inpatient ward — people pressing those floors tend to look more serious. I noticed a detail: in hospital elevators, people proactively help others press floors and hold doors. Perhaps it's because in this place, everyone more easily understands what it means to be vulnerable.

questions.q_zh: 医院电梯的按钮为什么比较低？
questions.q_en: Why are the hospital elevator buttons lower?

options.text: 为了坐轮椅的人能够得到
options.pinyin: wèi le zuò lúnyǐ de rén néng gòu de dào
options.text_en: So wheelchair users can reach them

questions.q_zh: 哪一层楼有最温暖的时刻？
questions.q_en: Which floor has the warmest moments?

questions.q_zh: 作者认为人们为什么在医院电梯里更愿意帮助别人？
questions.q_en: Why does the author think people are more willing to help in hospital elevators?

options.text: 因为每个人都更容易理解脆弱
options.pinyin: yīnwèi měi gè rén dōu gèng róngyì lǐjiě cuìruò
options.text_en: Because everyone more easily understands vulnerability
Because the elevator is too crowded

options.text: 因为医生告诉他们要这样做
options.pinyin: yīnwèi yīshēng gàosu tāmen yào zhèyàng zuò

title: The Missing Umbrella
text_zh: 办公室里有一个很小的谜。茶水间的伞架上本来有六把公用的透明雨伞，但是每到下雨天就会少一两把，等到晴天又会多出来。有时候多出来的不是原来那把——颜色不对，或者伞柄的形状不一样。我仔细观察了两个星期，发现了规律：每次少掉的伞，总是在两三天以后由不同的人带回来。我问了其中一个同事，她有点不好意思地说「那天雨太大了，我就先拿了一把，第二天忘了还，后来我自己的伞找到了，就把自己的放回去了」。原来消失的伞并没有真的消失，它们只是在公司和每个人的家之间旅行。伞架变成了一个小小的信任系统——没有登记本，没有监控，只靠大家的自觉。偶尔有一把伞永远消失了，但也偶尔会多出一把新的。整个系统保持着一种微妙的平衡。
text_pinyin: Bàngōngshì lǐ yǒu yí gè hěn xiǎo de mí. Cháshuǐ jiān de sǎn jià shàng běnlái yǒu liù bǎ gōngyòng de tòumíng yǔsǎn, dànshì měi dào xià yǔ tiān jiù huì shǎo yì liǎng bǎ, děng dào qíng tiān yòu huì duō chūlái. Yǒu shíhou duō chūlái de bú shì yuánlái nà bǎ — yánsè bú duì, huòzhě sǎn bǐng de xíngzhuàng bù yíyàng. Wǒ zǐxì guānchá le liǎng gè xīngqī, fāxiàn le guīlǜ: měi cì shǎo diào de sǎn, zǒng shì zài liǎng sān tiān yǐhòu yóu bùtóng de rén dài huílái. Wǒ wèn le qízhōng yí gè tóngshì, tā yǒudiǎn bù hǎoyìsi de shuō「nà tiān yǔ tài dà le, wǒ jiù xiān ná le yì bǎ, dì èr tiān wàng le huán, hòulái wǒ zìjǐ de sǎn zhǎo dào le, jiù bǎ zìjǐ de fàng huíqù le」. Yuánlái xiāoshī de sǎn bìng méiyǒu zhēn de xiāoshī, tāmen zhǐ shì zài gōngsī hé měi gè rén de jiā zhī jiān lǚxíng. Sǎn jià biàn chéng le yí gè xiǎoxiǎo de xìnrèn xìtǒng — méiyǒu dēngjì běn, méiyǒu jiānkòng, zhǐ kào dàjiā de zìjué. Ǒu'ěr yǒu yì bǎ sǎn yǒngyuǎn xiāoshī le, dàn yě ǒu'ěr huì duō chū yì bǎ xīn de. Zhěnggè xìtǒng bǎochí zhe yì zhǒng wēimiào de pínghéng.
text_en: There's a tiny mystery in the office. The umbrella rack in the tea room originally had six shared transparent umbrellas, but on rainy days one or two would disappear, then reappear on sunny days. Sometimes the ones that came back weren't the originals — wrong color, or different handle shape. I observed carefully for two weeks and found the pattern: each missing umbrella was always brought back two or three days later by a different person. I asked one colleague, and she said a bit sheepishly, 'it was raining so hard that day so I grabbed one, forgot to return it the next day, then found my own umbrella and put mine back instead.' So the missing umbrellas hadn't really disappeared — they were just traveling between the office and everyone's homes. The umbrella rack had become a small trust system — no sign-out sheet, no surveillance, just everyone's self-discipline. Occasionally one umbrella vanished forever, but occasionally a new one appeared. The whole system maintained a delicate balance.

questions.q_zh: 伞架上的伞什么时候会变少？
questions.q_en: When do the umbrellas decrease?

questions.q_zh: 同事为什么放回了一把不同的伞？
questions.q_en: Why did the colleague return a different umbrella?

options.pinyin: gùyì huàn le yì bǎ xīn de
options.text_en: Deliberately swapped for a new one

options.text: 找到了自己的伞，就把自己的放回去了
options.pinyin: zhǎo dào le zìjǐ de sǎn, jiù bǎ zìjǐ de fàng huíqù le
options.text_en: Found her own umbrella and put hers back instead

options.pinyin: wàng le shì nǎ yì bǎ

questions.q_zh: 作者认为伞架是什么样的系统？
questions.q_en: What kind of system does the author think the umbrella rack is?

options.pinyin: yí gè xiǎoxiǎo de xìnrèn xìtǒng

title: Speaking Two Languages at Home
text_zh: 我和妈妈说话用方言，和爸爸说话用普通话。这件事情我从来没有觉得奇怪，直到有一天朋友来我家吃饭，惊讶地问「你怎么跟你妈说一种话，跟你爸说另一种？」我才意识到这确实有点特别。妈妈是南方人，从小跟我说她老家的方言。爸爸是北方人，一直说普通话。他们两个人之间用普通话交流。所以在我们家的饭桌上，经常能听到两种语言来回切换。我说方言的时候声音会自然变软，用词也比较随意，因为方言对我来说意味着亲近和放松。说普通话的时候我会稍微正式一点，就像换了一个频道。有时候我找不到一个方言词的普通话说法，就会愣住。两种语言在我身体里和平共存，各自负责不同的情感领域。我不是两个人，但我确实有两个表达自己的入口。
text_pinyin: Wǒ hé māma shuōhuà yòng fāngyán, hé bàba shuōhuà yòng pǔtōnghuà. Zhè jiàn shìqing wǒ cónglái méiyǒu juéde qíguài, zhídào yǒu yì tiān péngyou lái wǒ jiā chīfàn, jīngyà de wèn「nǐ zěnme gēn nǐ mā shuō yì zhǒng huà, gēn nǐ bà shuō lìng yì zhǒng?」wǒ cái yìshí dào zhè quèshí yǒudiǎn tèbié. Māma shì nánfāng rén, cóng xiǎo gēn wǒ shuō tā lǎojiā de fāngyán. Bàba shì běifāng rén, yìzhí shuō pǔtōnghuà. Tāmen liǎng gè rén zhī jiān yòng pǔtōnghuà jiāoliú. Suǒyǐ zài wǒmen jiā de fànzhuō shàng, jīngcháng néng tīng dào liǎng zhǒng yǔyán lái huí qiēhuàn. Wǒ shuō fāngyán de shíhou shēngyīn huì zìrán biàn ruǎn, yòng cí yě bǐjiào suíyì, yīnwèi fāngyán duì wǒ lái shuō yìwèi zhe qīnjìn hé fàngsōng. Shuō pǔtōnghuà de shíhou wǒ huì shāowēi zhèngshì yìdiǎn, jiù xiàng huàn le yí gè píndào. Yǒu shíhou wǒ zhǎo bú dào yí gè fāngyán cí de pǔtōnghuà shuōfǎ, jiù huì lèng zhù. Liǎng zhǒng yǔyán zài wǒ shēntǐ lǐ hépíng gòngcún, gèzì fùzé bùtóng de qínggǎn lǐngyù. Wǒ bú shì liǎng gè rén, dàn wǒ quèshí yǒu liǎng gè biǎodá zìjǐ de rùkǒu.
text_en: I speak dialect with my mom and Mandarin with my dad. I never found this strange until one day a friend came to dinner and asked in surprise, 'how come you speak one language with your mom and another with your dad?' Only then did I realize it was a bit unusual. Mom is from the south and spoke her hometown dialect with me since I was small. Dad is from the north and has always spoken Mandarin. The two of them communicate in Mandarin. So at our dinner table you can often hear two languages switching back and forth. When I speak dialect my voice naturally softens and my word choice becomes more casual, because dialect means intimacy and relaxation to me. When I speak Mandarin I'm slightly more formal, like switching to a different channel. Sometimes I can't find the Mandarin equivalent of a dialect word and freeze. The two languages coexist peacefully inside me, each responsible for different emotional territories. I'm not two people, but I do have two doorways for expressing myself.

questions.q_zh: 爸爸和妈妈之间用什么语言交流？
questions.q_en: What language do the parents use with each other?

questions.q_zh: 作者说方言对自己意味着什么？
questions.q_en: What does the author say dialect means to them?

questions.q_zh: 作者怎样看待自己的双语状态？
questions.q_en: How does the author view their bilingual state?

options.text: 两种语言和平共存，各负责不同情感
options.pinyin: liǎng zhǒng yǔyán hépíng gòngcún, gè fùzé bùtóng qínggǎn
options.text_en: Two languages coexist peacefully, each handling different emotions

title: The Group Chat Name
text_zh: 我们大学寝室四个人有一个微信群，这个群的名字已经改了不下三十次。一开始叫「407寝室」，很正常。后来有人改成「四个找不到对象的人」，大家都笑了。再后来变成了「学术讨论组」——其实从来没有讨论过学术。毕业以后改成了「社会的毒打接受中心」，因为大家都开始上班了。有人结婚以后改成了「三个单身一个叛徒」。后来第二个人也结婚了，就变成了「二比二，公平竞争」。最近一次改名是上个月，因为四个人终于约好了一起吃饭，群名临时改成了「本周六火锅，谁迟到谁买单」。吃完火锅以后没有人改回去。我发现，这个群名的变化其实记录了我们四个人的人生阶段。将来如果有人写我们的传记，只要看群名的历史就够了。
text_pinyin: Wǒmen dàxué qǐnshì sì gè rén yǒu yí gè wēixìn qún, zhège qún de míngzi yǐjīng gǎi le bú xià sānshí cì. Yì kāishǐ jiào「sì líng qī qǐnshì」, hěn zhèngcháng. Hòulái yǒu rén gǎi chéng「sì gè zhǎo bú dào duìxiàng de rén」, dàjiā dōu xiào le. Zài hòulái biàn chéng le「xuéshù tǎolùn zǔ」— qíshí cónglái méiyǒu tǎolùn guò xuéshù. Bìyè yǐhòu gǎi chéng le「shèhuì de dúdǎ jiēshòu zhōngxīn」, yīnwèi dàjiā dōu kāishǐ shàngbān le. Yǒu rén jiéhūn yǐhòu gǎi chéng le「sān gè dānshēn yí gè pàntú」. Hòulái dì èr gè rén yě jiéhūn le, jiù biàn chéng le「èr bǐ èr, gōngpíng jìngzhēng」. Zuìjìn yí cì gǎi míng shì shàng gè yuè, yīnwèi sì gè rén zhōngyú yuē hǎo le yìqǐ chīfàn, qún míng línshí gǎi chéng le「běn zhōuliù huǒguō, shéi chídào shéi mǎidān」. Chī wán huǒguō yǐhòu méiyǒu rén gǎi huíqù. Wǒ fāxiàn, zhège qún míng de biànhuà qíshí jìlù le wǒmen sì gè rén de rénshēng jiēduàn. Jiānglái rúguǒ yǒu rén xiě wǒmen de zhuànjì, zhǐyào kàn qún míng de lìshǐ jiù gòu le.
text_en: The four of us from our college dorm have a WeChat group that's been renamed no fewer than thirty times. It started as '407 Dorm' — perfectly normal. Then someone changed it to 'Four People Who Can't Find Partners' and everyone laughed. Later it became 'Academic Discussion Group' — though no academics were ever discussed. After graduation it became 'Society's Beating Reception Center' because everyone started working. When one person got married it became 'Three Singles and One Traitor.' When the second person married, it changed to 'Two vs Two, Fair Competition.' The most recent rename was last month when the four of us finally managed to schedule dinner together — the group was temporarily renamed 'Hot Pot This Saturday, Whoever's Late Pays.' After the hot pot, nobody changed it back. I realized the group name changes actually documented our life stages. If someone wrote our biography in the future, they'd only need to look at the name history.

questions.q_zh: 群名改了大约多少次？
questions.q_en: Roughly how many times has the group name been changed?

questions.q_zh: 「三个单身一个叛徒」是什么意思？
questions.q_en: What does 'Three Singles and One Traitor' mean?

options.pinyin: yí gè rén líkāi le qún
yí gè rén bān zǒu le
yí gè rén huàn le gōngzuò

questions.q_zh: 作者觉得群名变化记录了什么？
questions.q_en: What does the author think the name changes record?

options.pinyin: sì gè rén de rénshēng jiēduàn

title: Grandma's Pickled Vegetables
text_zh: 奶奶有一个泡菜坛子，比我的年龄还大。坛子是暗红色的，肚子圆圆的，口很小，坛沿有一圈水槽，盖上盖子以后水会把空气隔绝在外面。奶奶说这个坛子是她结婚的时候外婆送的，里面的泡菜水从来没有换过——只是不断地加盐、加水、加新的蔬菜。所以这坛泡菜的味道已经发酵了几十年。每次去奶奶家，她都会打开坛子，用干净的筷子捞出几根萝卜或者几片白菜。泡菜的味道酸酸咸咸的，比任何餐厅的都好吃。我问奶奶秘诀是什么。她想了想说「没有秘诀，就是时间」。后来我试着自己做泡菜，买了新坛子，按照奶奶教的步骤，一个月以后味道还是差很远。我才明白，有些味道不是技术的问题，而是需要几十年的耐心和一个从来不中断的过程。
text_pinyin: Nǎinai yǒu yí gè pàocài tánzi, bǐ wǒ de niánlíng hái dà. Tánzi shì ànhóng sè de, dùzi yuányuán de, kǒu hěn xiǎo, tán yán yǒu yì quān shuǐcáo, gài shàng gàizi yǐhòu shuǐ huì bǎ kōngqì géjué zài wàimiàn. Nǎinai shuō zhège tánzi shì tā jiéhūn de shíhou wàipó sòng de, lǐmiàn de pàocài shuǐ cónglái méiyǒu huàn guò — zhǐ shì búduàn de jiā yán, jiā shuǐ, jiā xīn de shūcài. Suǒyǐ zhè tán pàocài de wèidào yǐjīng fājiào le jǐ shí nián. Měi cì qù nǎinai jiā, tā dōu huì dǎkāi tánzi, yòng gānjìng de kuàizi lāo chū jǐ gēn luóbo huòzhě jǐ piàn báicài. Pàocài de wèidào suānsuān xiánxián de, bǐ rènhé cāntīng de dōu hǎochī. Wǒ wèn nǎinai mìjué shì shénme. Tā xiǎng le xiǎng shuō「méiyǒu mìjué, jiù shì shíjiān」. Hòulái wǒ shì zhe zìjǐ zuò pàocài, mǎi le xīn tánzi, ànzhào nǎinai jiāo de bùzhòu, yí gè yuè yǐhòu wèidào háishi chà hěn yuǎn. Wǒ cái míngbai, yǒuxiē wèidào bú shì jìshù de wèntí, ér shì xūyào jǐ shí nián de nàixīn hé yí gè cónglái bù zhōngduàn de guòchéng.
text_en: Grandma has a pickle jar that's older than I am. It's dark red with a round belly and small mouth, with a water groove around the rim — once the lid is on, the water seals air out. Grandma says her mother gave it to her when she got married, and the pickling brine inside has never been replaced — she just keeps adding salt, water, and fresh vegetables. So the flavor of these pickles has been fermenting for decades. Every time I visit grandma, she opens the jar and fishes out a few radishes or cabbage leaves with clean chopsticks. The pickles taste sour and salty, better than any restaurant's. I asked grandma what the secret was. She thought for a moment and said 'there's no secret, just time.' Later I tried making my own pickles, bought a new jar, followed grandma's steps exactly — a month later the taste was still far off. Then I understood: some flavors aren't a matter of technique but require decades of patience and a process that's never been interrupted.

questions.q_zh: 泡菜坛子是谁送给奶奶的？
questions.q_en: Who gave grandma the pickle jar?

questions.q_zh: 奶奶说泡菜的秘诀是什么？
questions.q_en: What does grandma say is the secret?

questions.q_zh: 作者自己做的泡菜怎么样？
questions.q_en: How did the author's own pickles turn out?

title: The Balcony Garden
text_zh: 对面楼的五楼有一个阳台花园。我不认识那个住户，但每天早上能看到一个穿围裙的女人出来浇花。她的阳台不大，但种满了各种植物——有的开花，有的结果，有的只是绿叶。花盆排列得很整齐，大的在后面，小的在前面，每一盆都能晒到太阳。我注意到她浇花的方式很特别：不是从左到右按顺序，而是先浇看起来最渴的那几盆，然后再浇其他的。她浇完花会站在那里看一会儿，有时候用手摸一摸叶子，好像在确认它们今天的状态。有一次台风过后，我看到她阳台上几盆花倒了，泥土撒了一地。第二天一早她就在那里收拾，把每一盆重新整理好。一个星期以后阳台又恢复了原来的样子。我觉得那个阳台不只是一个花园——它是一个人对生活的态度的缩影：认真、耐心、不轻易放弃。
text_pinyin: Duìmiàn lóu de wǔ lóu yǒu yí gè yángtái huāyuán. Wǒ bú rènshi nàge zhùhù, dàn měi tiān zǎoshang néng kàn dào yí gè chuān wéiqún de nǚrén chūlái jiāo huā. Tā de yángtái bú dà, dàn zhǒng mǎn le gè zhǒng zhíwù — yǒu de kāi huā, yǒu de jiéguǒ, yǒu de zhǐ shì lǜ yè. Huāpén páiliè de hěn zhěngqí, dà de zài hòumiàn, xiǎo de zài qiánmiàn, měi yì pén dōu néng shài dào tàiyáng. Wǒ zhùyì dào tā jiāo huā de fāngshì hěn tèbié: bú shì cóng zuǒ dào yòu àn shùnxù, ér shì xiān jiāo kàn qǐlái zuì kě de nà jǐ pén, ránhòu zài jiāo qítā de. Tā jiāo wán huā huì zhàn zài nàlǐ kàn yíhuìr, yǒu shíhou yòng shǒu mō yì mō yèzi, hǎoxiàng zài quèrèn tāmen jīntiān de zhuàngtài. Yǒu yí cì táifēng guò hòu, wǒ kàn dào tā yángtái shàng jǐ pén huā dǎo le, nítǔ sā le yí dì. Dì èr tiān yì zǎo tā jiù zài nàlǐ shōushi, bǎ měi yì pén chóngxīn zhěnglǐ hǎo. Yí gè xīngqī yǐhòu yángtái yòu huīfù le yuánlái de yàngzi. Wǒ juéde nàge yángtái bù zhǐ shì yí gè huāyuán — tā shì yí gè rén duì shēnghuó de tàidù de suōyǐng: rènzhēn, nàixīn, bù qīngyì fàngqì.
text_en: On the fifth floor of the building across from mine there's a balcony garden. I don't know the resident, but every morning I can see a woman in an apron come out to water her plants. Her balcony isn't large but it's packed with all kinds of plants — some flowering, some bearing fruit, some just green leaves. The pots are arranged neatly, large ones in back, small ones in front, each getting sunlight. I noticed her watering method is special: not left to right in order, but first watering whichever pots look thirstiest, then the rest. After watering she stands and looks for a while, sometimes touching the leaves, as if checking on their condition today. Once after a typhoon I saw several pots knocked over on her balcony, soil scattered everywhere. The next morning she was already there cleaning up, reorganizing every pot. A week later the balcony looked the same as before. I think that balcony isn't just a garden — it's a miniature of one person's attitude toward life: careful, patient, not easily giving up.

questions.q_zh: 女人浇花的顺序是什么？
questions.q_en: In what order does the woman water her plants?

options.pinyin: xiān jiāo kàn qǐlái zuì kě de

questions.q_zh: 台风以后发生了什么？
questions.q_en: What happened after the typhoon?

options.pinyin: tā bú zài zhòng huā le

options.text: 几盆花倒了，她第二天收拾好了
options.pinyin: jǐ pén huā dǎo le, tā dì èr tiān shōushi hǎo le
options.text_en: Some pots fell over, she cleaned up the next day

questions.q_zh: 作者认为阳台花园代表了什么？
questions.q_en: What does the author think the balcony garden represents?

options.text: 对生活认真、耐心、不轻易放弃的态度
options.pinyin: duì shēnghuó rènzhēn, nàixīn, bù qīngyì fàngqì de tàidù
options.text_en: An attitude of care, patience, and not giving up easily toward life
